elements.sent-2411-001: Thorson Resource Ecology and Fisheries Management, Alaska Fisheries Science Center, National Marine Fisheries Service, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration \fi \if0\blind { \begin{center { \bf A note on numerical evaluation of conditional Akaike information for nonlinear mixed-effects models \end{center \fi.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose two methods to evaluate the conditional Akaike information (cAI) for nonlinear mixed-effects models with no restriction on cluster size.
elements.sent-2411-003: Method 1 is designed for continuous data and includes formulae for the derivatives of fixed and random effects estimators with respect to observations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Method 2, compatible with any type of observation, requires modeling the marginal (or prior) distribution of random effects as a multivariate normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-005: Simulations show that Method 1 performs well with Gaussian data but struggles with skewed continuous distributions, whereas Method 2 consistently performs well across various distributions, including normal, gamma, negative binomial, and Tweedie, with flexible link functions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on our findings, we recommend Method 2 as a distributionally robust cAI criterion for model selection in nonlinear mixed-effects models.
elements.sent-2411-007: Nonlinear mixed-effects models (NMMs) are widely used across disciplines such as medicine, public health, pharmacology, fisheries, and ecology, where the quantities of interest are nonlinear functions of both fixed and random effects \citep{lindstrom1990nonlinear .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In these models, the log-joint likelihood of the data ($y$) and random effects (REs, $\Psi$) conditional on the fixed effects (parameters, $\theta$) can be expressed as $\log{ f(y,\Psi|\theta) = \log{ f(y|\theta,\Psi) + \log{ f(\Psi|\theta)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Maximum likelihood estimation of $\theta$ is achieved by optimizing the log-marginal likelihood, $\log{ f(y|\theta)}$, which results from integrating out the REs from the joint density.
elements.sent-2411-010: Practitioners of NMMs often need to select the optimal model from a wide range of alternatives \citep[e.g.,][]{cadigan2022complex , with many relying on the conventional Akaike information criterion \citep[AIC,][]{Akaike1973AIC .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: AIC is intended to estimate the expected log-marginal likelihood for out-of-sample predictions, where the degree of freedom correction adjusts for the expected difference between in-sample and out-of-sample prediction \citep[termed ``model optimism" in][]{hooten2015guide .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, \cite{vaida2005conditional pointed out that when the REs are of primary interest, rather than merely a device for modeling the correlation among responses, the Akaike Information (AI) and the corresponding model selection criterion should be redefined using the log-conditional likelihood, $\log{ f(y|\hat{\theta , \hat{\Psi )}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These are termed conditional AI (cAI) and conditional AIC (cAIC), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-014: Here and throughout the paper, the hat notation denotes estimators of parameters or predictors of REs.
elements.sent-2411-015: Studies also suggest that the conventional AIC is too conservative for mixed-effects model selection, while the cAIC performs more reliably in this context \citep[e.g.][]{yu2012conditional,wood2016smoothing .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given the strong relevance of cAIC to mixed-effects model applications, its formulation and evaluation have been extensively studied across various settings, including linear mixed-effects models with independent Gaussian observations of known variance \citep{liang2008note , generalized linear mixed models with known dispersion \citep{yu2012conditional , and smooth models \citep{wood2016smoothing , among others.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the lack of a unified cAIC evaluation methodology makes it difficult for practitioners of NMMs to identify if there are appropriate cAIC formulae in the literature that correspond to their specific research settings.
elements.sent-2411-018: This challenge is further compounded by the development of advanced modeling tools like TMB \citep{kristensen2016tmb , which enable the exploration of NMMs that do not easily fit into the existing cAIC frameworks.}
elements.sent-2411-019: On the other hand, some existing cAIC methods may be further generalized and improved using these advanced modeling tools.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, \cite{liang2008note suggests evaluating their cAIC using numerical approximations based on small perturbations of the observed data, which can be challenging to implement in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Leveraging R \citep{r2018citation and TMB's strong automatic differentiation (up to order three) capabilities, we propose a simpler method in Sec.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This example also highlights our aim: to explore cAIC evaluation using recent advances in numerical methods and corresponding inferential theory for NMMs \citep{zheng2021frequentist,Zheng2021P2 .}

elements.sent-2411-152: Thus, \begin{linenomath* \begin{equation}\label{Elc_August_4_2024}  E\left\lbrace l_c(\hat{\phi},\hat{\Psi}|y)\right\rbrace = E\left\lbrace l_c(\Theta_o|y)\right\rbrace + E\left\lbrace (\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)^{\top}\dfrac{\partial l_c(\Theta_o|y)}{\partial\Theta_o}\right\rbrace +E\left\lbrace \dfrac{1}{2}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)^{\top}\dfrac{\partial^2 l_c(\Theta_o|y)}{\partial\Theta_o\partial\Theta_o^{\top}}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)\right\rbrace = E\left\lbrace l_c(\Theta_o|y)\right\rbrace + p_c + q - trace\left\lbrace \ddot{l}^{-1}_j(\phi_o,\tau_o,\Psi_o|y)\ddot{l}_r(\tau_o,\Psi_o)\right\rbrace + E\left\lbrace \dfrac{1}{2}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)^{\top}\dfrac{\partial^2 l_c(\Theta_o|y)}{\partial\Theta_o\partial\Theta_o^{\top}}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)\right\rbrace + O(1/n_t). \end{equation} \end{linenomath* The bias for using $-2\,l_c(\hat{\phi ,\hat{\Psi |y)$ to estimate cAI is then \begin{linenomath* \begin{equation}\label{Elc_August_4_2024}  -2\,E\left\lbrace l_c(\hat{\phi},\hat{\Psi}|y)\right\rbrace - cAI = -2\,E\left\lbrace l_c(\Theta_o|y)\right\rbrace -2\, p_c -2\,q + O(1/n_t) +2\, trace\left\lbrace \ddot{l}^{-1}_j(\phi_o,\tau_o,\Psi_o|y)\ddot{l}_r(\tau_o,\Psi_o)\right\rbrace -E\left\lbrace (\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)^{\top}\dfrac{\partial^2 l_c(\Theta_o|y)}{\partial\Theta_o\partial\Theta_o^{\top}}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)\right\rbrace +2\,E_{g(y,u)}\left[ E_{g(y^*|u)}\left\lbrace l_c(\phi_o,\Psi_o|y^*)\right\rbrace + \dfrac{1}{2}(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o)^{\top}E_{g(y^*|u)}\left\lbrace\dfrac{\partial^2 l_c(\Theta_o|y^*)}{\partial\Theta_o\partial\Theta_o^{\top}}\right\rbrace(\hat{\Theta} - \Theta_o) \right] = -2\, p_c -2\,q +2\, trace\left\lbrace\ddot{l}^{-1}_j(\phi_o,\tau_o,\Psi_o|y) \ddot{l}_r(\tau_o,\Psi_o)\right\rbrace + O(1/n_t). \end{equation} \end{linenomath* Here we neglect the difference between the second order terms.}}}}}}
